title: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)
steps: Ensure the scene is safe for both you and the victim.
Check for responsiveness and normal breathing (tap and shout).
Call emergency services immediately and request an AED (Defibrillator).
Place the heel of one hand in the center of the chest with the other hand on top.
Perform high-quality compressions: 2-2.4 inches (5-6 cm) deep, at a rate of 100-120 per minute.
Allow full chest recoil between compressions; do not lean on the chest.
Continue until professional help arrives, an AED is ready, or the person becomes responsive.

title: Severe External Hemorrhage
steps: Wear gloves if available to avoid contact with bodily fluids.
Apply firm, direct pressure to the source of bleeding with sterile gauze or clean cloth.
If blood soaks through, do not remove the dressing; apply another one on top.
Apply a pressure bandage to maintain constant force on the wound.
If life-threatening bleeding on an extremity persists, apply a commercial tourniquet 2-3 inches (5 cm) above the wound.
Treat for shock: lay the person flat and keep them warm.

title: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver / 5-and-5)
steps: If the person is coughing forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing.
If they cannot speak, cough, or breathe, apply '5-and-5' approach (AHA 2026 standard).
Give 5 back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
If object isn't cleared, perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich): place fist above navel and pull inward and upward.
Continue alternating 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts.
If the person becomes unresponsive, lower them to the ground and start CPR immediately.

title: Thermal Burns
steps: Stop the burning process and cool the area with cool or lukewarm running water for 10-20 minutes.
Do NOT use ice, butter, or ointments, as they can damage tissue further.
Remove jewelry or tight clothing before the area starts to swell (unless stuck to the skin).
Cover the burn loosely with sterile non-stick dressing or plastic wrap.
Do not pop blisters; they act as a natural barrier against infection.
Seek urgent medical attention if the burn is large, deep, or on the face/hands.

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)
steps: Perform FAST assessment: Face (is it drooping?), Arms (can they lift both?), Speech (is it slurred?), Time (call now).
Note the exact time symptoms started for the stroke team.
Call emergency services immediately and state 'Stroke Alert'.
Keep the person calm and do not give them anything to eat or drink.
If they lose consciousness but are breathing, place them in the recovery position (lateral).
